title: Omid Mirzamohammadi
text:
I am a Ph.D. researcher in cryptography at COSIC, KU Leuven, under the supervision of Prof. Bart Preneel, Prof. Dave Singelée, and Dr. Aysajan Abidin. My work focuses on designing cryptographic protocols for privacy-preserving systems.



I am interested in provable security and the formal analysis of cryptographic protocols. My current research focuses on anonymous credentials, privacy threats in decentralized systems, and post-quantum cryptography. I am particularly interested in designing new constructions and rigorously proving their security.

title: Research
text: My research is about building cryptographic protocols that protect user privacy while remaining efficient enough for real-world systems.

title: Research
text:
Anonymous credentials
Anonymous credentials allow a user to prove statements about certified attributes without revealing all of their information. For example, a user may prove that they are over a certain age or belong to an institution without revealing their full identity.
I am especially interested in efficient credential presentations, selective disclosure, and multi-show unlinkability.

title: Research
text:
Keyed-verification anonymous credentials
In keyed-verification anonymous credentials, verification is performed using a secret verification key rather than only public information. This model is useful in settings where the issuer and verifier have a trust or key-sharing relationship, and it can lead to very efficient constructions.
My work studies how to make these systems efficient, privacy-preserving, and suitable for partial disclosure.

title: Research
text:
Decentralized identity and Solid
Decentralized identity systems aim to give users more control over their data. However, they also introduce privacy challenges, such as identity linking and repeated tracking across services.
I study cryptographic tools that can help reduce these privacy risks while preserving useful functionality.

title: Research
text:
Privacy-preserving computation
More broadly, I am interested in cryptographic methods that allow parties to compute, verify, or authenticate information while revealing as little as possible.
